chapters.title: Teach your child to be antiracist and you change the future
chapters.text:
Babies are not born racist. Children do not wake up one day with opinions about people of color unless they have soaked up those opinions from the people around them. Racism is taught, not inherited, and the same is true of antiracism.

Racists are made, not born. That means an antiracist can be made too.

As a parent, you hold a great deal of influence. You can teach your child to see the beauty in human differences and to treat those differences as something to celebrate rather than something to fear. You can show them that the color of a person skin does not change their value.

There is no neutral ground. A child is either nudged toward racist thinking or away from it. With the steps in this piece, you can help your child grow up in a world that treats everyone fairly. If every parent took the same approach, the change across generations would be enormous.

A 2018 review of police stops in New York City found that about eighty eight percent of those stopped were Black or Latino, while only ten percent were white. Roughly seventy percent of those stops involved innocent people.

chapters.title: Help your child see the full range of skin tones
chapters.text:
Pretending that skin color does not exist is just as harmful as treating it as a problem. Children need to understand that people come in many shades, and that the shade of a person skin has nothing to do with how they should be treated.

Talk openly about skin color and help your child find the beauty in the variety around them.

A person is measured by what they do and how they treat others, not by the color of their skin. Encourage your child to notice the wide range of skin tones in the world and to appreciate the differences they see.

Claiming to be color blind, Ibram X Kendi has written, is a way of denying what is right in front of you.

chapters.title: Speak up about racism
chapters.text:
Racism is a global problem. Staying quiet about it, or letting racist moments pass without comment, sends the message that it does not matter. It does matter. It matters a great deal.

A big part of raising an antiracist child is showing them that racism exists, that it is harmful, and that it has to stop. Use your words when you see something racist. Talk about what you see and hear, and help your child understand why those thoughts or actions are wrong.

Children need to see the harm racism causes and to understand why things have to change.

Racism has to be named, called out, and discussed. If we do not do this work as a community and as parents, it will keep being just as violent and destructive as it has been for centuries.

The conversation can feel heavy, and it is fair to wonder whether such a topic is appropriate for a child. If you want your child to be antiracist, though, you have to teach them what racism is and why it is wrong from an early age.

A US study found that job applications with names that sound Black were roughly fifty percent less likely to get a callback than applications with names that sound traditionally white.

chapters.title: Policies, not just people, shape inequality
chapters.text:
It is a fact that some people get more of what they want, while others get less. The gap is not always about effort or luck. The rules in place often decide who gets more and who gets less, and that is not a fair system. It is a racist and prejudiced one.

Policies frequently target people of color and minority groups in unfair ways.

The problem is not the people so much as the policies that govern them. These rules push certain groups, often people of color, into positions where they have to work ten times harder for a quarter of the reward.

Teach your child that the world is not always fair, and that policies can be stacked against certain groups. Show them that people of color are often the ones hurt by these rules. The antiracist babies raised today may grow up to push for changes that finally bring justice and fairness to everyone.

chapters.title: Under the surface, we are all the same
chapters.text:
Ibram X Kendi argues that children should learn to see and value differences in skin color and culture, and that they should also learn that under all those differences, we are simply human.

Help your child see that we are all different, and that underneath those differences we are all human.

We are born the same way. We live our lives, and we die. The only difference is what happens in between. We all have a heart that beats, blood that moves, a brain that thinks, and feelings that matter.

Wherever a person is born, whatever color their skin, whatever culture they belong to, whatever language they speak, they are still a person. Beneath everything, we are the same.

chapters.title: Celebrate what makes us different
chapters.text:
Diversity is something to enjoy. It is a way to learn something new. Encourage your child to lean into differences, to learn about cultures they have not seen, to pick up words in other languages, and to ask questions.

The antiracist baby does not think any group is better or worse. They love a world that is truly diverse, as Ibram X Kendi has written.

Teaching a child from a young age that we are all human and all equal pushes back against the racist idea that some groups are worth more than others.

Help your child find the wonder in different cultures. Show them dances, songs, and languages from around the world and let them react with curiosity rather than judgment. Every culture is unique, beautiful, and just as important as the next.

Encourage your child to learn about new cultures, art, and languages, and to enjoy the experience.

chapters.title: Admit mistakes and learn from them
chapters.text:
Being antiracist means being honest about the times you have said something racist or thought in a racist way. Questioning long held beliefs is one of the foundations of breaking racism down. Learning and changing takes courage, and it is how a more open society gets built.

Teach your child that admitting a racist thought or word is the first step toward doing better.

As your child grows, encourage them to own up to the moments when they have thought or said something racist. Talk through why it was wrong, and help them understand. The way to push racism out of our lives is through understanding and education.

The point is not to make a child feel guilty for every slip, but to teach them and help them see the mistake. Children are still learning, and the racist world they live in will leave a mark sometimes. The goal is to give them the strength and the courage to speak up and learn from what they got wrong.

Racism grows when we do not question our thoughts and words. Education is the lever that moves the world.

chapters.title: Encourage your child to stay curious
chapters.text:
The antiracist baby is always learning, changing, and growing, as Ibram X Kendi has written.

As a parent, you can encourage your child to be curious about the people and the world around them. Curiosity makes it easy to ask questions, pick up new ideas, and grow into an open minded adult.

There is a great deal to learn from other cultures, and a curious child will keep finding new things to explore and new people to meet. A curious child learns to be antiracist because they treat differences as a source of wonder rather than a reason to be afraid.

Encourage your child to ask questions and to meet new people.

chapters.title: Look beyond the surface
chapters.text:
The antiracist baby believes that, in time, we will beat racism. They do not judge a book by its cover. They get to know the person as a fellow human being, treat them with equal respect, and wait to see the whole story before forming an opinion.

Raising a child to understand racism gives real hope that the problem can be solved. The antiracist baby is a key part of a major cultural shift.

The antiracist baby will be part of the reason racism finally ends. They bring an attitude of curiosity, they celebrate differences, they call out racism when they see it, and they understand that the problem is not people in general but the policies that shape the system.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Racism has been part of human history for as long as records exist, and it has done enormous harm. If we want to root it out, we have to start with how we raise our children. We have to raise a generation of antiracist babies who will grow up and challenge unfair policies, celebrate differences, and break the cycle.

Our children really are the future, and teaching them that racism does not belong in the world is a real step toward ending the oppression and discrimination that has hurt people for centuries.

Practical steps include talking with your child about skin color and cultural differences and inviting their questions. Silence lets racism grow. Read books and watch shows that highlight different cultures. When something about racism comes up in the news, be honest about what happened and resist the urge to soften the story.

Keep reading to the end.
